serving.natural_language_portion: One whole chilled young coconut typically yields approximately 240 ml (240 g) of coconut water, served directly from the shell with a straw.

nutrient_source.title: Coconut water, raw — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 170174)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for raw coconut water used as primary nutrient reference. Values represent the liquid endosperm of young coconuts, which is the product served. Chilling does not materially alter nutrient composition.

gi_source.title: Coconut water, GI value from peer-reviewed literature — Livesey et al. and University of Sydney GI Database analogous reference
gi_source.source_match_notes: Coconut water has a reported GI of approximately 54 (low GI) based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for coconut water and corroborating peer-reviewed literature (Trinidad et al., 2010, Diabetes Care). GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (54 × 3.71 × 2.4) / 100 ≈ 5.3 for a 240 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: High potassium content with moderate sodium
hypertension.guidance: Fresh coconut water provides approximately 250 mg potassium and 105 mg sodium per 100 g, giving a favourable potassium-to-sodium ratio that may support blood pressure regulation; individuals on potassium-restricted diets due to renal complications of hypertension should monitor intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and modest carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 54 and only 3.71 g carbohydrate per 100 g, fresh coconut water has a low glycemic load per serving and is generally suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in standard single-coconut portions; larger volumes should be accounted for in daily carbohydrate budgets.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat, zero cholesterol, and electrolyte profile
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Fresh coconut water is very low in saturated fat (0.18 g/100 g), contains no cholesterol, and provides potassium and magnesium which are associated with cardiovascular health; it is a heart-friendly hydration choice when consumed unsweetened and without added syrups.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fresh Coconut Water (Chilled) is the clear, slightly sweet liquid endosperm extracted from young green coconuts (Cocos nucifera), served cold or over ice. In Singapore and Malaysia it is commonly sold at hawker centres, food courts, and via food delivery platforms, often presented in the whole coconut shell with a straw. The liquid is naturally refreshing with a mild sweetness, subtle nuttiness, and a light electrolyte-rich flavour profile. It is minimally processed — the coconut is simply cracked or cut and chilled before serving. Nutritionally, coconut water is low in calories (approximately 19 kcal per 100 g), low in fat (0.2 g/100 g), and provides a meaningful amount of potassium (250 mg/100 g), making it a popular natural sports and hydration drink. Its glycemic index is approximately 54 (low GI), and a standard serving of one whole coconut (~240 ml) delivers a glycemic load of around 5, making it suitable for most individuals including those managing Type 2 Diabetes. The sodium content of 105 mg/100 g is moderate and the potassium-to-sodium ratio is favourable for blood pressure management. Coconut water is vegan, gluten-free, dairy-free, and halal. It is culturally significant across Southeast Asia as a traditional cooling beverage and is widely consumed in Singapore, Malaysia, Thailand, and Indonesia.
common_user_queries: Is fresh coconut water good for diabetics?
How many calories in a fresh coconut?
Is coconut water high in sugar?
Is coconut water good for high blood pressure?
What is the GI of coconut water?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 170174) for raw coconut water, which is an exact match to the product. GI value corroborated by University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature. Multilingual names verified against standard lexicons for Singaporean context.
notes: polyunsaturated_fat_g is 0.00 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 170174); the value is below the reporting threshold and listed as 0 in the source. trans_fat_g is 0.00 — naturally zero as coconut water is a raw plant liquid with no industrial hydrogenation. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a plant-derived beverage. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy; coconut water contains no significant beta-carotene or retinol. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero as vitamin B12 is not present in plant foods. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero; coconut water contains no vitamin D. vitamin_e_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy for coconut water liquid. GI of 54 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database and Trinidad et al. (2010) peer-reviewed data for coconut water. The input record listed cuisine_tags including Bak Kut Teh, Pork Dish, and Soup, which are artefacts of the source restaurant context (Legendary Bak Kut Teh) and do not describe the food itself; these tags have been discarded. The food_group has been corrected from Mixed to Beverages. The food_name has been normalised from the promotional menu string to the canonical form.
